text:
There once was a wise old owl who lived in an ancient oak tree by a tranquil pond. Every morning, he would glide gracefully from his lofty perch to bath in the clear water. One day, a young squirrel named Fern visited the oak tree.

Fern had heard tales of the wise old owl from her woodland friends. She had always admired how he knew so much about the forest—and especially the depths of the pond.

One afternoon, Fern asked the owl, "How do you know so much?"

The wise owl sighed deeply. "Knowledge isn't something you acquire; it's something you hold close.")

Fern listened intently as the owl spoke of the songs of birds, the whispers of leaves, and the silent wisdom of ancient trees.

After some time, the owl said softly, "But I do know one thing. I know joy."

With those words, he flew away, leaving Fern puzzled.

For days after that conversation, Fern searched the forest for signs of happiness. She found
